terms.definition: A function f where f(t x + (1-t) y) <= t f(x) + (1-t) f(y) for all x, y and t in [0, 1]; its graph lies below every chord. Equivalently (when twice differentiable on R^n), the Hessian is positive semidefinite everywhere.
A function that is not convex; the chord property fails somewhere, allowing multiple local minima, saddles, and initialization-dependent optimization paths.
A point x* where f(x*) <= f(x) for all x in some neighborhood of x*; may not be the smallest value globally when the objective is non-convex.
A point x* where f(x*) <= f(x) for every feasible x; for convex problems over a convex domain, any local minimum is also global.
A critical point (gradient zero) where the Hessian is indefinite—curvature is positive in some directions and negative in others—so the point is neither a local minimum nor a local maximum.
A symmetric matrix whose eigenvalues are all >= 0; for twice-differentiable f, the Hessian being positive semidefinite everywhere characterizes convexity.
